intake.finding: Well-scoped interoperability/correctness fix, not a new API (feature_type 'No developer-visible change', category CSS, Blink>Scroll). The ChromeStatus record and its motivation clearly frame the problem: Blink propagated overscroll-behavior from <body> to the viewport while Firefox/WebKit propagate from <html>, so the spec's own motivating example (html { overscroll-behavior-y: contain }) silently failed in Chrome. The underlying tracking bug was filed in 2019 by an external web developer and confirmed as a bug by the overscroll-behavior author (majidvp), establishing a genuine, long-standing developer problem rather than a vendor-preference change.
intake.limitations: No public explainer/design doc; the feature relies on the spec and the tracking bug for problem framing.

incubation.finding: Problem and solution shape were well-established before shipping. The CSS Working Group resolved on 2021-04-08 (F2F) to propagate scrollbar-gutter from root, to NOT propagate scrollbar-gutter from body, that 'no future properties should propagate from <body> to the ICB or viewport', and to 'deprecate any existing use of body propagation'; during that discussion iank_ noted 'Overscroll behavior does [propagate from body]' and emilio that scroll-snap/similar 'only work on the root'. A follow-up CSSWG issue (#6406, 2021) explicitly asked whether non-auto overscroll-behavior should propagate from either root or body and listed affected sites (Spotify, Reddit mobile, Tumblr, TikTok, Redfin, Tinder, Tesla, Pinterest); that issue remained Open, so Chrome shipped against the existing spec (root-only) rather than a newly-amended one.
incubation.limitations: CSSWG issue #6406's 'either root or body' question was unresolved at ship, leaving a latent spec-design question that the launch did not close.

prototype.finding: Implementation and measurement instrumentation predate this ChromeStatus entry by years. UseCounter/UMA for the observable-behavior-difference case was added in late 2020 (CL e70da10, Nov 26 2020) and refined in 2021 (split into two cases: html-auto/body-non-auto = would-break vs html-non-auto/body-auto = would-fix, CL 20ef9da May 18 2021). The property author (majidvp) identified the fix location (document.cc viewport propagation logic, related to crbug 952711) as early as 2019. The fix path was therefore understood well before the 2025 ChromeStatus entry.
prototype.limitations: No public explainer or design doc is linked from ChromeStatus; prototype rationale lives in the bug and code review.

developerTrials.finding: No formal developer trial or cohort was run (no devtrial stage; feature_type is 'No developer-visible change'). The risk substitute was a manual inspection of 20 of the most-affected popular sites (bit.ly/4ecLqQi), comparing Chrome's behavior with and without the fix against Firefox/Safari, concluding the change is low-risk. This is a reasonable substitute for a behavior fix with no new API surface, but it is a 20-site author-side inspection, not a representative developer cohort, and it is the primary basis for the 'low risk' claim.
developerTrials.limitations: The 20-site inspection document (bit.ly/4ecLqQi) could not be retrieved (likely access-restricted Google Doc), so the methodology and site list could not be independently verified.

wideReview.finding: Broad standards and cross-engine alignment existed and was actively reinforced. CSSWG resolved on the propagation principle (2021 F2F). Firefox (Gecko) already propagates only from root and Mozilla engineers engaged directly in the Chromium bug (emilio/bb...@mozilla, Oct 2023) clarifying that Chrome ignores overscroll-behavior:contain on <html>. Safari/WebKit are recorded as 'Shipped/Shipping' for the behavior, and a WebKit bug (240183) shows WebKit actively working overscroll-behavior semantics. The topic was accepted as an Interop 2025 'Web compat' focus area with a dedicated proposal ('overscroll-behavior on the root scroller'), providing shared WPT tests and cross-vendor accountability. TAG review was recorded 'Not applicable', consistent with a spec-aligned behavior fix.
wideReview.limitations: 'No signals' from web developers is recorded on ChromeStatus; the developer signal that exists is the original 2019 bug report rather than attributable demand for the change at ship time.

experiment.finding: No Origin Trial was run and none was warranted: this is a behavior-correctness fix for an already-shipped, Baseline property (overscroll-behavior shipped Chrome 63/Firefox 59/Safari 16), not a new API needing real-origin field evidence. The runtime-flag (Finch 'PropagateOverscrollBehaviorFromRoot') gating, the long-running UseCounter, and the manual 20-site inspection served the risk-assessment role that an OT would otherwise fill. Marked not-relevant because a field experiment would not have answered a question the UseCounter/manual-inspection evidence could not.
experiment.limitations: Not-relevant here means no OT was justified; it does not mean the behavior change was untested (it was gated behind a flag and UseCounter-assessed).

prepareToShip.finding: Prepare-to-ship artifacts were coherent and transparent. A 'Web-Facing Change PSA' was posted to blink-dev (thread an611rpPxLQ) by the owner (awogbemila) listing spec, tracking bug, WPT results link, Finch name, rollout plan ('Will ship enabled for all users'), platform support (all six Blink platforms), and explicit interop risk framing. Enterprise was notified from milestone 139 (first_enterprise_notification_milestone=139), consistent with an enterprise_impact=1 (low) change that nonetheless alters observable gesture behavior. The ChromeStatus record documents the UseCounter (>1% of page loads) and the 20-site manual inspection transparently rather than hiding the compat risk.
prepareToShip.limitations: The PSA's estimated milestones were Desktop/Android/WebView 139, but the feature shipped in 140 (see release phase) — a one-milestone slip not reconciled in the PSA text.

release.finding: Shipped enabled-by-default in Chrome 140 stable (release date September 2, 2025) across Desktop, Android, and WebView (iOS null). The Chrome 140 release notes include an accurate, concise section explaining the root-vs-body propagation change and that it makes Chrome 'comply with the specification and become interoperable with other implementations'. Chrome Enterprise and Education release notes also document the change. MDN's overscroll-behavior page (last modified 2026-04-20) was updated so its guidance/examples target the root (<html>) element, reflecting the shipped behavior. The fix was enabled via CL 6732448 ('Enable overscroll-behavior fix', Jul 14 2025) and the bug marked Fixed Aug 28 2025.
release.limitations: Actual ship milestone (140) is one later than the PSA estimate (139); the slip is minor and not material to the outcome, but indicates the PSA's milestone estimate was not updated.

adoption.finding: As a behavior fix to an existing property, 'adoption' maps to absence of meaningful breakage rather than uptake of a new API. No independent post-launch breakage, regression, or WebCompat reports attributable to this change were found across targeted searches ~10 months after stable. The cached CSS property-family UseCounter (bucket 585) shows the underlying overscroll-behavior property fired on 0.1445% of measured Chrome page loads at M140 stable (2025-09-02), drifting within 0.1422%–0.1941% across the day30/day90/day180 checkpoints and the latest point (2026-07-21) — a low, slowly-rising exposure surface consistent with the pre-launch low-risk assessment. This counter measures exposure to the property, not adoption of the root-propagation fix, and absence of found breakage reports is not affirmative evidence of zero breakage.
adoption.limitations: The cached counter is property-family (any use of overscroll-behavior), not the specific would-break case (popularity/3736) behind the '>1%' caution, whose current value is not in the cache. Low/rising property exposure reduces but does not eliminate the inferred compat ceiling, and no independent breakage/regression evidence confirms zero breakage.

support.finding: No support issues, support-pack artifacts, or recurring question patterns specifically attributable to this behavior change were located. The tracking bug is closed as Fixed with no open regression sub-issues visible. The feature has no dedicated docs/sample links on ChromeStatus (consistent with 'No developer-visible change'), so there is no public support surface to assess.
support.limitations: Support burden for a behavior fix is often invisible in public trackers; absence of found issues is not evidence of low burden.

deprecation.finding: This feature is not a Chrome deprecation or removal. The only deprecation framing is at the standards level: the CSSWG resolved (2021) to 'deprecate any existing use of body propagation' as a general principle, but Chrome shipped a behavior alignment, not a removal path, reverse trial, or enterprise policy. There is no deprecation calendar entry, no removal milestone, and no migration toolkit to assess.
deprecation.limitations: Not-relevant means deprecation process does not apply; the latent CSSWG 'deprecate body propagation' principle could surface as future guidance work but did not gate this launch.

developerValue.finding: The change addresses a real, validated, long-standing developer problem: Chrome's body-propagation made the spec's own motivating example (overscroll-behavior on <html>) fail silently since the property shipped in Chrome 63 (2017). The original report was filed by an external developer in 2019 citing the spec, the property author confirmed it as a bug, and the divergence caused documented cross-engine confusion (Gecko bug discussions referenced). Aligning Chrome to root-propagation makes the property behave as authored across all three engines.
developerValue.limitations: ChromeStatus records 'No signals' from web developers at ship; the developer value is evidenced primarily through the original bug and cross-engine standards work rather than broad attributable demand at launch.

endUserImpact.finding: The change alters observable end-user gesture behavior: pull-to-refresh (mobile) and forward/backward swipe navigation (desktop) may begin or stop occurring on pages where overscroll-behavior was set on <body> but not <html>. The owner's pre-launch manual inspection of 20 popular sites assessed this as low-risk and noted the new behavior mostly matches Firefox/Safari. The cached CSS property-family UseCounter (bucket 585) bounds the exposure surface: the gesture-affecting property is present on a low ~0.14%–0.19% of measured Chrome page loads around and after launch — but property presence is not a measurement of pull-to-refresh/swipe-nav incidence, and no post-launch end-user telemetry or regression reports were found, so end-user impact cannot be defensibly scored.
endUserImpact.limitations: Pre-launch risk assessment is owner-authored (20-site inspection, not independently reproduced); the cached counter bounds the exposure surface but does not measure gesture-incidence changes. Not scored per the rule against inferring success from absence of criticism.

adoption.finding: For a behavior-correctness fix there is no exact adoption counter — the change is silent behavior, not a new API — and no predeclared adoption target was recorded. The closest available measurement is the cached CSS property-family UseCounter (bucket 585, scope property-family, confidence name-matched-family), which records the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads on which the overscroll-behavior property fired at least once. At M140 stable (2025-09-02) the property fired on 0.1445% of page loads; at the launch-relative checkpoints it was 0.1422% at day30 (2025-10-02), 0.1592% at day90 (2025-12-01), and 0.1779% at day180 (2026-03-01), reaching 0.1941% at the latest point (2026-07-21) against a series peak of 0.2087% (2026-07-11) — a low, slowly-rising level across ~10 months post-stable. This is exposure to the underlying property, not adoption of the root-propagation fix: the counter cannot distinguish html-set from body-set usage and does not measure whether the new propagation behavior is relied upon. The specific would-break behavior-difference counter (popularity/3736) that motivated the pre-launch '>1%' caution is a different instrument and is not in the cache. No post-stable breakage or regression evidence was found, consistent with the low-risk assessment but not affirmative proof. With no exact fix counter, no predeclared target, and no production/retention/breakage corroboration, adoption is kept unscored and the measured level/trend described rather than scored as success or mixed.
adoption.limitations: The cached counter is property-family (broader than the fix) and not directly comparable to the would-break behavior-difference counter (popularity/3736, historically ~2%/0.38%/0.82%/>1% per the bug thread and ChromeStatus record). Percentages are share of measured page loads where the counter fired — not unique developers/sites/users, task success, satisfaction, or end-user benefit. Kept unscored (not success/mixed) per the absent-target rule.

interoperability.finding: This is the feature's explicit and achieved goal. After the fix, Chrome propagates overscroll-behavior from the root like Firefox (Gecko) and Safari (WebKit), closing a multi-year three-engine divergence. The topic was accepted into Interop 2025 as part of the 'Web compat' focus area with a dedicated proposal and WPT coverage (overscroll-behavior-root.html), Mozilla engineers engaged constructively in the bug, and the CSSWG resolution underpins the chosen behavior. The underlying property is Baseline Widely Available (since 2025-03-12); this fix removes the last engine-specific quirk in its viewport semantics.
interoperability.limitations: Specific WPT pass-rate numbers per engine could not be captured from the live wpt.fyi dashboard (JS-rendered); Interop 2025 inclusion and the referenced test confirm coverage exists.

implementationQuality.finding: The fix landed cleanly: three code reviews (CL 6546636 'Propagate overscroll-behavior from the root element to the viewport', May 14 2025; CL 6635849 'Fully fix propagating overscroll-behavior from root', Jun 11 2025; CL 6732448 'Enable overscroll-behavior fix', Jul 14 2025) by the owner (David Awogbemila), reviewed by Blink>Scroll owners. It shipped behind a Finch flag (PropagateOverscrollBehaviorFromRoot) for rollout control, is covered by a referenced WPT test, and the risk was assessed via a long-running UseCounter plus targeted manual inspection rather than asserted. The bug was closed Fixed (Aug 28 2025) with no visible open regressions.
implementationQuality.limitations: Independent WPT pass-rate verification and any post-stable regression scan could not be performed from public dashboards via automated retrieval.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: Launch communication was accurate, consistent, and transparent across channels. ChromeStatus, the blink-dev PSA, the Chrome 140 developer release notes, and the Chrome Enterprise release notes all describe the same root-vs-body change and cite the CSSWG resolution. The ChromeStatus record transparently discloses the >1% UseCounter and the manual 20-site inspection rather than presenting the change as risk-free. MDN guidance was updated to target the root element, matching shipped behavior. The only blemish is a minor milestone-estimate slip (PSA said 139; shipped 140).
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: The 20-site inspection methodology underlying the 'low risk' public claim is not independently verifiable (access-restricted doc).

support.finding: No support artifacts, known-issues material, or recurring-issue patterns attributable to this change were located, and the feature has no dedicated public docs/samples on ChromeStatus (consistent with 'No developer-visible change'). The tracking bug is closed with no visible open regression. Without a public support surface or post-launch issue data, support burden cannot be defensibly assessed.
support.limitations: Support load for silent behavior fixes is often absorbed in private channels; absence of public issues is not evidence of low support burden.

overall.finding: A successful interoperability outcome on a clean, well-instrumented implementation, with accurate cross-channel communication — but an outcome assessment that cannot be called an unqualified success because the end-user-impact, adoption, and support dimensions lack independent post-launch evidence. The core goal (three-engine alignment, reinforced by Interop 2025 and a CSSWG resolution) was achieved on a 6-year-old, externally-reported, author-confirmed bug. The now-cached property-family UseCounter (bucket 585) shows a low, slowly-rising exposure surface for the underlying property (~0.1445% of page loads at stable, ~0.1941% ten months later), which is consistent with the low-risk assessment but does not measure fix adoption or confirm absence of breakage; the residual risk dimensions therefore remain unscored rather than confirmed-safe.
overall.limitations: Overall is mixed because success is established for interoperability/implementation/communication but unscored for end-user impact, adoption, and support; the method prohibits inferring overall success from shipment, usage, or absent criticism. The cached property-family percentage is exposure, not fix adoption.

supports: Feature metadata, ship milestones (Desktop/Android/WebView 140), finch name PropagateOverscrollBehaviorFromRoot, UseCounter >1% + 20-site manual inspection, ff/safari=Shipped, webdev=No signals, spec link, enterprise notification M139
sources.limitations: Cached record accurate_as_of 2025-07-28; the CSS property-family counter (bucket 585) is now cached separately (src-metrics-css-585), while the specific behavior-difference UseCounter (popularity/3736) live value remains JS-rendered and not independently captured.

supports: CSS property-family UseCounter (bucket 585, overscroll-behavior; scope property-family, confidence name-matched-family): the property fired on 0.1445% of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads at M140 stable (2025-09-02), 0.1422% at day30 (2025-10-02), 0.1592% at day90 (2025-12-01), 0.1779% at day180 (2026-03-01), 0.1941% latest (2026-07-21), with a series peak of 0.2087% (2026-07-11); first observed positive 2017-08-19. Timeline series cached at evidence/metrics/timelines/css-585.json (SHA-256 9c6eea5c365909dcb8cdb5a3e4ea284e49d6af59a7e7e5da87ad49cdefec2b0e).
sources.limitations: Scope is property-family (any use of the overscroll-behavior CSS property), NOT exact adoption of the root-propagation behavior change; the counter cannot distinguish html-set from body-set usage or whether the fix's behavior is relied upon. Counter was renamed from scroll-boundary-behavior to overscroll-behavior (same bucket 585, continuous series). Percentages are the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads where the counter fired at least once — not unique developers/sites/users, successful tasks, satisfaction, or end-user benefit. UMA reflects participating Chrome clients; enterprise and opt-out populations may be underrepresented.

supports: Spec defines overscroll-behavior and its motivating example using html { overscroll-behavior-y: contain }, which Chrome's body-propagation violated
sources.limitations: Working Draft maturity (val 4); specific propagation normative text not re-extracted in this retrieval.

supports: 'Web-Facing Change PSA' for the launch; spec/tracking bug/WPT link; Finch name; rollout 'ship enabled for all users'; estimated milestones Desktop/Android/WebView 139; interop risk framing
sources.limitations: Exact post date not extracted from the page; estimated ~2025-06 from feature creation date. This is a PSA, not a full Intent to Ship.

supports: Prior 2021 Intent to Ship for the same fix (old feature 5734614437986304, crbug 954423) was put ON HOLD by API owner Daniel Bratell because ~2% page-load impact was too high and could not be driven down; documents the earlier abandoned attempt and the reason
sources.limitations: Historical (2021) attempt; superseded by the 2025 PSA, but explains the multi-year delay.

supports: Bug filed 2019-04-19 by external developer jonjohnjohnson; confirmed a bug by property author majidvp (2019); UseCounter added 2020-2021; usage trajectory ~2%(2021)/0.38%(Oct2021)/0.82%(Jun2023)/>1%(2025); Mozilla engagement Oct 2023 (emilio/bb...@mozilla); fixed 2025 by David Awogbemila via CLs 6546636/6635849/6732448; Status Fixed, P2, S4, 9 votes, Hotlist-Interop; OS Android/Mac/ChromeOS
sources.limitations: Full comment thread read via browser snapshot; some Monorail-migration comments empty. Migrated from crbug 954423 in Jan 2024. The ~2%/0.38%/0.82%/>1% figures are the would-break behavior-difference UseCounter (popularity/3736), a different instrument from the cached CSS property-family counter (src-metrics-css-585).

supports: Chromium Interop-2025 tracking issue for the same fix ('We have a fix which is behind an [experimental flag]')
sources.limitations: Full page could not be retrieved (reader network error); claim from search-result snippet only.

sources.publisher: W3C CSS Working Group (minutes by Dael Jackson)
supports: CSSWG Virtual F2F 2021-04-08 Overflow agenda: RESOLVED propagate scrollbar-gutter from root to viewport; RESOLVED scrollbar-gutter does not propagate from <body>; RESOLVED no future properties propagate from <body> to ICB/viewport; RESOLVED deprecate any existing use of body propagation. During discussion iank_ noted 'Overscroll behavior does [propagate from body]' and emilio that scroll-snap etc. 'only work on the root'.
sources.limitations: Resolutions were on scrollbar-gutter/general propagation principle; the specific overscroll-behavior resolution referenced in the feature (issue 6079 comment 816307011) is this same F2F's general body-propagation decision.

supports: Open CSSWG issue asking whether non-auto overscroll-behavior should propagate from either root or body; lists affected sites (open.spotify.com, reddit.com mobile, tumblr.com, tiktok.com, redfin.com, tinder.com, tesla.com, pinterest.com); confirms the long-tail compat concern Chrome accepted by shipping root-only
sources.limitations: Issue body only (reader did not extract comments); status Open at retrieval, i.e. the spec-design question remains unresolved.

sources.publisher: web-platform-tests Interop project (Apple, Bocoup, Google, Igalia, Microsoft, Mozilla)
supports: Interop 2025 'Web compat' focus area explicitly includes 'overscroll-behavior on the root scroller' with a dedicated focus-area proposal and tests, selected on evidence of real-world developer/end-user divergence
sources.limitations: README does not state per-engine pass rates; focus-area acceptance confirms the divergence is recognized industry-wide.

supports: Issue documenting the root-scroller implementation divergence: 'Gecko only respects it on the <html> element. Blink only respects it on [the body]'
sources.limitations: Reader returned page chrome rather than full comment thread; core claim captured from issue description/metadata.

supports: Focus-area proposal 'overscroll-behavior on the root scroller for inclusion in Interop 2025' — 'accepted as part of the Web Compat focus area'
sources.limitations: Claim captured from search-result snippet; full issue not directly read.

supports: overscroll-behavior property is Baseline Widely Available since 2025-03-12 (Chrome 63, Firefox 59, Safari 16); included in Interop 2022 and 2023
sources.limitations: Baseline applies to the property's existence, not specifically to this root-propagation correctness fix, which post-dates the Baseline 'widely available' date.

supports: MDN guidance/examples now target the root element (html { overscroll-behavior: none }) consistent with the shipped behavior; notes iframe guidance to set on both html and body
sources.limitations: MDN page last modified after the M140 ship date; reflects post-ship docs state.

supports: Chrome 140 stable release date September 2, 2025; dedicated section 'Propagate viewport overscroll-behavior from root' stating the change makes Chrome 'comply with the specification and become interoperable with other implementations'; tracking bug #41453796
sources.limitations: Release-notes entry is concise; does not quantify breakage risk to end users.

sources.publisher: Google Chrome Enterprise and Education Help
supports: Enterprise release notes document the overscroll-behavior root-vs-body change, consistent with first_enterprise_notification_milestone 139 and enterprise_impact=1
sources.limitations: Exact enterprise-note publication/revision date not captured.

supports: WebKit actively working overscroll-behavior semantics ('CSS overscroll-behavior-x: contain does not disable history navigation'), corroborating that the property's gesture semantics are a live cross-engine concern
sources.limitations: Found via search; full bug not read. Tangential to the root-propagation change but evidences WebKit engagement with the property.

successes: Resolved a six-year (2019–2025) three-engine interoperability divergence that the property's own author had confirmed was a bug, achieving the feature's explicit goal: Chrome now propagates overscroll-behavior from the root like Firefox and Safari.

Strong, transparent risk instrumentation: a long-running UseCounter plus a targeted 20-site manual inspection (with the >1% figure disclosed on ChromeStatus) substituted appropriately for an Origin Trial on a behavior fix to an already-Baseline property.

Accurate, consistent cross-channel communication: ChromeStatus, the blink-dev PSA, Chrome 140 developer release notes, Chrome Enterprise release notes, and an updated MDN page all describe the same change and cite the CSSWG resolution.

Industry reinforcement: the change shipped into an accepted Interop 2025 'Web compat' focus area with shared WPT coverage and constructive Mozilla engagement, rather than as a unilateral Chrome decision.
failures: Outcome evidence is thin for the risk dimensions: ~10 months after stable there is still no independent post-launch breakage/regression, user-impact, or support-burden data, so end-user-impact and support cannot be defensibly scored. Adoption is now partially illuminated by the cached property-family UseCounter (bucket 585: ~0.1445% of page loads at stable, ~0.1941% ten months later — a low, slowly-rising exposure surface) but remains unscored for outcome because the counter is broader than the fix, there is no predeclared target, and there is no production/retention/breakage corroboration.

A latent spec-design question (CSSWG issue #6406, 'propagate non-auto overscroll-behavior from either root or body') remained open at ship; Chrome shipped against the existing spec rather than resolving the ambiguity, leaving a residual risk for the long-tail of sites that set the property on <body>.
Minor process inconsistency: the blink-dev PSA estimated ship at milestone 139 but the feature shipped in 140; the estimate was not reconciled in the PSA.
counterfactuals: The 2021 attempt was correctly put on hold when the UseCounter showed ~2% page-load impact that could not be driven down; shipping then without Interop 2025's forcing function and cross-engine alignment could have caused widespread, hard-to-attribute changes to pull-to-refresh and swipe-navigation gestures. The ~4-year wait was defensible risk management, not inaction.

If the team had driven the open CSSWG question (#6406, either-root-or-body) to a resolution before shipping, the long-tail compat risk for body-set sites (Spotify, Reddit mobile, Tumblr, TikTok, etc.) could have been removed by spec change rather than accepted — at the cost of delaying alignment further.

If a fix-specific post-launch monitoring plan had been defined at ship — telemetry on pull-to-refresh/swipe-nav incidence and a WebCompat/crbug regression watch — this retrospective could have scored end-user impact and breakage rather than leaving them unscored. Standing property-family CSS telemetry (bucket 585) now exists incidentally, but it measures property presence, not gesture-incidence or breakage; its absence-as-a-plan is still the main thing preventing an unqualified success verdict.

Mozilla's 2023 question (is the html-propagation half low-risk even if the body-removal half is not?) suggests a staged option — propagate from html first, deprecate body later — that could have de-risked rollout, though it would have prolonged divergence.
skillImprovements: Add a lifecycle rule for 'No developer-visible change' features that nonetheless alter observable behavior (gestures, navigation, focus): treat them as behavior changes requiring a pre-ship risk assessment AND a defined post-stable monitoring plan, not as invisible fixes. This feature's end-user impact remains unscored precisely because no such monitoring was evident.

For high-UseCounter behavior fixes, require a refreshed reading of the RELEVANT counter taken close to ship date and require the record to state why the current value is acceptable, with the inspection methodology reproducible (the 20-site doc here is access-restricted and unverifiable). This feature now illustrates the scope trap: the cached CSS property-family counter (bucket 585) shows the underlying property at ~0.1445% of page loads at stable, while the would-break behavior-difference counter (popularity/3736) read '>1%' on ChromeStatus — different instruments that must not be conflated, and the would-break counter (the one that actually motivated caution) is still not captured as a current value.

Add a phase check: do not ship a behavior change against an open spec-design question (CSSWG #6406) without either resolving it or explicitly recording the accepted residual risk and the affected-site class.

Add a release-comms accuracy check that reconciles PSA/intent milestone estimates with the actual ship milestone (PSA said 139, shipped 140) and updates the estimate, so historical launch records stay accurate.

Add a retrospective evidence step that captures post-stable signals 1–2 milestones after a behavior change lands. The metrics cache now supplies one of these — the property-family UseCounter delta (bucket 585: ~0.1445% at stable → ~0.1779% at day180) — but the step must additionally capture the fix-specific would-break counter (popularity/3736), crbug/WebCompat regressions, and support-forum themes, so end-user impact and adoption can be scored rather than left unknown.

missingEvidence: Current value of the specific would-break behavior-difference UseCounter (chromestatus.com/metrics/feature/timeline/popularity/3736, the html-auto/body-non-auto case behind the '>1%' caution) — not in the metrics cache; only its historical values from the bug thread and the ChromeStatus-recorded '>1%' are available. The broader CSS property-family counter (bucket 585) IS now cached (src-metrics-css-585).
Post-launch independent breakage/regression evidence (crbug, WebCompat, support forums) attributable to the M140 change — none located ~10 months after stable; absence is not affirmative evidence.
End-user-impact telemetry (pull-to-refresh / swipe-navigation incidence changes) — not publicly available; the cached counter measures property presence, not gesture incidence.
WPT pass-rate numbers per engine for overscroll-behavior-root.html from wpt.fyi — dashboard is JS-rendered; only test existence and Interop-2025 coverage are confirmed.
Contents of the 20-site manual inspection document (bit.ly/4ecLqQi) — access-restricted Google Doc; methodology and site list could not be independently verified.
Full text of Chromium Interop issue 399102343 — reader returned a network error; only a search-snippet claim is available.
